content.text: Integrating contract tests into CI pipelines turns the PACT workflow from a developer-run local check into an automated, enforced gate that blocks incompatible changes from reaching any shared environment. The integration involves four distinct CI events — consumer test run, pact publication, provider verification, and can-i-deploy gate — each of which must be wired to the correct pipeline stage to provide continuous API contract protection.
What it is

Contract test CI integration is the practice of embedding PACT consumer test execution, pact publication, provider verification, and can-i-deploy enforcement into the existing CI/CD pipeline so that API contract compliance is checked automatically on every code change. Rather than relying on developers to manually run contract tests, the pipeline enforces them as mandatory gates that a change must pass before it can be deployed to staging or production.

The integration adds four jobs to a typical CI pipeline: a consumer contract test job that generates pact files and publishes them to the Pact Broker; a provider contract verification job triggered either by the consumer's publication webhook or on a schedule; a can-i-deploy check that runs before any deployment job; and a record-deployment step that informs the Broker which version of each service is in which environment.

Because the consumer and provider are typically in separate repositories with separate CI pipelines, contract test CI integration spans two pipelines and requires both to be configured. The Pact Broker is the coordination point that connects the two pipelines via webhooks and shared state.
Why it matters

Without CI integration, contract tests provide no deployment protection. A developer might run consumer tests locally, generate pacts, but forget to publish them. The provider might run verification on a schedule but not on every commit. The can-i-deploy check might be omitted from the deployment pipeline. Any of these gaps breaks the contract testing guarantee: changes that violate the contract still reach production.

CI integration closes each gap with automation. Publishing is triggered on every commit to main or on every PR. Provider verification is triggered by a webhook when a new pact arrives. can-i-deploy runs as the last pre-deployment gate and is not skippable. The record-deployment step updates the Broker's environment map so future can-i-deploy checks know what is actually running in each environment.

For agentic AI systems, the consequences of missing a contract check are particularly costly. A broken tool API boundary in a multi-step agent loop does not produce a simple HTTP error; it produces a confusing mid-workflow failure that may not be reproducible or traceable to its root cause without detailed trace logging. CI contract enforcement is the cheapest point at which to catch these failures.
Benefits
Automated enforcement: no human needs to remember to run contract tests or publish pacts — the pipeline does it.
Cross-team coordination: the webhook-triggered provider verification notifies the provider team when a consumer introduces a new expectation.
Environment-aware protection: can-i-deploy checks which services are deployed in the target environment, not just whether a pact exists.
Full audit trail: the Pact Broker records every pact publication, every verification result, and every deployment event — providing a queryable history of API contract status.
Fail-fast: incompatible changes are blocked before they reach a shared environment, not after someone notices a production incident.
Independent deployment: once verified, consumer and provider can deploy at different times without coordination, trusting the contract check.
Trade-offs
Consumer and provider pipelines must both be configured, which requires cross-team coordination during initial setup.
Webhook-triggered provider verification adds pipeline complexity; misconfigured webhooks can cause silent verification gaps.
The Pact Broker must be highly available because every deployment pipeline depends on it for the can-i-deploy check.
Record-deployment steps must be maintained in every deployment pipeline; missing them causes can-i-deploy to use stale environment information.
Pending pact configuration requires careful tuning — too aggressive and new pacts immediately block providers; too lenient and new contracts go unverified indefinitely.
How it works
Consumer pipeline job

On every pull request, the consumer pipeline runs the contract test suite, which generates updated pact files. After a merge to main, a separate job publishes the pacts to the Pact Broker tagged with the commit SHA as the version and the branch name. The publish step uses the pact-broker publish CLI command with the broker URL and authentication token from CI secrets.
Broker webhook to provider pipeline

The Pact Broker is configured with a webhook that fires whenever a new pact is published by a consumer. The webhook calls the provider's CI pipeline API, triggering a provider verification run. This decouples the consumer and provider pipelines: the consumer does not wait for the provider verification; the provider is notified asynchronously and runs verification in its own pipeline.
Provider verification job

The provider pipeline runs a verification job that starts the provider application in a test harness, fetches all pacts from the Broker, replays them, and publishes verification results. The job is triggered by the webhook and also runs on every merge to the provider's main branch. If verification fails, the job exits with a non-zero code, failing the provider's CI.
can-i-deploy gate

The can-i-deploy check runs as a job in every deployment pipeline, before any deployment step. It queries the Pact Broker with the consumer (or provider) name and version being deployed, plus the target environment name. The Broker returns whether all pact relationships for that version have verified results for the services already deployed in that environment. A failure exits with a non-zero code, blocking the deployment.
record-deployment step

After a successful deployment, the pipeline runs pact-broker record-deployment to inform the Broker which version of the service is now live in which environment. This step is critical: can-i-deploy uses this information to determine what is deployed in the target environment. Skipping it causes can-i-deploy to use stale information and will produce incorrect results.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Contract test CI integration pipeline showing consumer tests, pact broker, provider verification, can-i-deploy gate, and deployment stages

content.text: The diagram shows the five-stage CI pipeline. The consumer contract test stage publishes pacts to the Pact Broker. The Broker sends a webhook to trigger provider verification. The can-i-deploy stage queries the Broker before the deploy stage. The Broker connects the consumer and provider pipelines without requiring direct inter-pipeline dependencies.
When to use it
Use full CI integration when consumer and provider deploy independently and cannot coordinate releases manually.
Use it when the tool service or LLM proxy is used by more than two consumers — manual contract verification does not scale.
Use it when the CI pipeline already has a clear pre-deployment gate structure that can accommodate a can-i-deploy step.
For single-team monorepos where consumer and provider are deployed together, a simplified version (local verification, no broker) may be sufficient.
How to implement

# .github/workflows/consumer-contract.yml (runs on push to main)
name: Consumer Contract Tests
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
jobs:
  consumer-contract:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      - name: Run consumer contract tests
        run: pytest tests/pact/ -v --tb=short
      - name: Publish pacts to broker
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: |
          npx pact-broker publish tests/pact/contracts/ \
            --consumer-app-version "${{ github.sha }}" \
            --branch "${{ github.ref_name }}" \
            --broker-base-url "${{ vars.PACT_BROKER_URL }}" \
            --broker-token "${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}"

# .github/workflows/provider-verification.yml
# Triggered by Pact Broker webhook when new consumer pacts are published
name: Provider Pact Verification
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      pact_url:
        description: "URL of the pact to verify (from broker webhook)"
        required: false
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  provider-verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      app:
        image: myorg/search-tool-service:${{ github.sha }}
        ports:
          - "8080:8080"
        env:
          DB_URL: "postgresql://test:test@localhost:5432/testdb"
          APP_ENV: "test"
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install -e ".[test]"
      - name: Wait for service to be ready
        run: |
          until curl -sf http://localhost:8080/health; do sleep 2; done
      - name: Run pact provider verification
        run: pytest tests/pact/provider_verification.py -v
        env:
          PACT_BROKER_URL: ${{ vars.PACT_BROKER_URL }}
          PACT_BROKER_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}
          PROVIDER_VERSION: ${{ github.sha }}
          PROVIDER_BRANCH: ${{ github.ref_name }}
          PACT_URL: ${{ inputs.pact_url }}

# Steps in deployment pipeline (added before deploy job)
      - name: can-i-deploy check
        run: |
          npx pact-broker can-i-deploy \
            --pacticipant "${{ env.SERVICE_NAME }}" \
            --version "${{ github.sha }}" \
            --to-environment "${{ env.DEPLOY_ENV }}" \
            --broker-base-url "${{ vars.PACT_BROKER_URL }}" \
            --broker-token "${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}"
      
      - name: Deploy to ${{ env.DEPLOY_ENV }}
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh ${{ env.DEPLOY_ENV }}
      
      - name: Record deployment
        if: success()
        run: |
          npx pact-broker record-deployment \
            --pacticipant "${{ env.SERVICE_NAME }}" \
            --version "${{ github.sha }}" \
            --environment "${{ env.DEPLOY_ENV }}" \
            --broker-base-url "${{ vars.PACT_BROKER_URL }}" \
            --broker-token "${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}"
Examples
Real-world example

An enterprise data platform operates an agent orchestrator (consumer) that calls six internal microservices (providers): a query service, a schema service, a lineage service, a classification service, a masking service, and a catalog search service. Each service has a separate repository and CI pipeline. Before contract test CI integration, the platform suffered API contract breaks every three to four sprints as services evolved independently.

After integrating contract tests into all seven CI pipelines, the team recorded zero API-contract-related production incidents in the subsequent eight months. The key configuration decisions were: pending pacts enabled on all providers so that new consumer pacts triggered provider verification notifications rather than immediate failures; record-deployment configured on all seven deployment pipelines with three environments (dev, staging, production); and can-i-deploy running as a required check on the staging and production deployment gates but informational only on dev.
Common mistakes
Publishing pacts only from the main branch, not from PRs — delays feedback until after merge; consider publishing from PRs too with the branch tag.
Forgetting the record-deployment step — causes can-i-deploy to use stale environment data and produce incorrect results.
Not configuring the broker webhook to trigger provider verification — forces provider teams to discover pact changes manually.
Using the same broker token across all pipelines — a compromised token allows any pipeline to publish pacts under any service name.
Not setting pending pacts to true on the provider — new consumer pacts immediately block provider CI before the provider team has a chance to implement the change.
Not recording provider deployments in addition to consumer deployments — can-i-deploy checks for both parties to the contract.
Best practices
Create one Pact Broker service account token per service with minimal permissions (publish pacts for this service only).
Configure the pending pacts feature on all providers to prevent new consumer pacts from being a surprise blocker.
Add can-i-deploy to staging and production deployment gates as a required step; make it informational-only for dev to allow experimentation.
Record deployments for every service in every environment after every successful deployment.
Monitor the Pact Broker's webhook delivery history — failed webhooks mean provider verification is not triggered and the contract is silently unverified.
Set a maximum wait time on the provider verification webhook trigger — if the provider has not verified within 30 minutes, treat it as a failure in the consumer's pipeline.
Performance & scalability

The consumer contract test job typically adds 30–120 seconds to a PR check. Pact publication is a fast HTTP POST — under five seconds. Provider verification depends on provider startup time and the number of interactions to replay; typical times are 30–120 seconds for 20–50 interactions. can-i-deploy is a single API call to the Broker — under five seconds.

At scale (dozens of services, hundreds of pact relationships), the Pact Broker's database must be sized appropriately. The Broker stores every version of every pact plus all verification results; a large installation may accumulate gigabytes of data over months. PactFlow offers automated cleanup of old pact versions; self-hosted Broker installations require periodic pruning of stale data.
Tooling & ecosystem
pact-broker CLI
: official CLI for publish, can-i-deploy, record-deployment, and webhook management.
PactFlow
: managed Pact Broker with enhanced features; SLA-backed uptime removes the broker availability burden.
GitHub Actions pact-action
: community GitHub Action for publishing pacts and running can-i-deploy checks.
Pact Broker Docker image
: official Docker image for self-hosting; runs on Postgres or SQLite.
pactflow-webhook-proxy
: optional proxy for webhook delivery to CI systems behind firewalls.
Comparison with alternatives

No

Security & reliability

The Pact Broker is a critical infrastructure dependency: every deployment pipeline depends on it for the can-i-deploy check. A Broker outage blocks all deployments. Mitigate this by deploying the Broker with high availability (multiple replicas behind a load balancer) and by configuring CI pipelines to fail open on Broker unreachability for non-production environments (can-i-deploy --no-fail-if-no-pacts flag).

Webhook payloads from the Pact Broker to provider CI systems should be authenticated using HMAC signatures or short-lived tokens to prevent spoofed verification triggers. Broker API tokens must be stored as encrypted CI secrets and rotated every 90 days. Audit logs of pact publications and verification results should be retained for at least 90 days to support incident investigation.
Key takeaways
Contract test CI integration turns PACT from a local developer tool into an automated deployment gate that blocks incompatible changes.
Four pipeline jobs must be configured: consumer test + publish, provider verification (webhook-triggered), can-i-deploy gate, and record-deployment.
The Pact Broker connects consumer and provider pipelines via webhooks without creating direct CI dependencies.
record-deployment is mandatory — omitting it causes can-i-deploy to use stale environment information.
Pending pacts allow new consumer expectations to notify rather than immediately block providers.
The Pact Broker is a critical infrastructure service; deploy it with high availability and monitor webhook delivery health.
Scoped per-service tokens and HMAC-authenticated webhooks are the minimum security controls for a production contract testing setup.
